column1: 2004-05-11вЂЋMy mother just finished reading Jane Eyre

column5: and it made my think of an aspect which many good novels share. ItГўв‚¬в„ўs the idea of putting a character in the story for means of comparing him to the main character
column6: or giving another perspective. Take Pride and Prejudice for example. Jane Austen frequently makes a statement about marriage with her novels. In Pride and Prejudice she highlights Lizzie and DarcyГўв‚¬в„ўs relationship by putting in couples like Mr. and Mrs.Bennet

column2: not only would we lose those wonderful good vs. evil plots

column1: 2004-05-11вЂЋAbout 1/3 of me is an aspiring writer (I won't say what the other 2/3's are though). I've had writer's block for a while
column2: at least a block for fiction. Anyway
column3: that block has
column4: thankfully
column5: been removed. A few weeks ago I had a great idea for a story
column6: which could easily work itself into a novel. Before I began any writing
column7: though
column8: I just thought about the plot for a few days. When some flesh had started to appear on the bones of the story
column9: I made a basic outline and plot summary. Then
column10: two days ago
column11: I started to work on writing the first chapter. I had the names of my characters and how they would change and develop over the course of the story
column12: but as I started to write
column13: I realized that though I knew where the characters were going
column14: I didn't know where they came from. In other words
column15: I knew how they would change over the course of the story
column16: but I didn't know what they would change from. So I have again put the writing part aside
column17: until I know the characters well enough to do justice to them. I've never read any books about writing a book
column18: or anything like that
column19: so I don't pretend to have any knowledge on the subject
column20: other than that which I've gained from my own personal experience
column21: and from what I've read of other's fiction. But I know that had I continued to write without really knowing who I was writing about
column22: I'd never be able to achieve any character development that would penetrate an audience.

column1: 2004-05-11вЂЋ"I was thinking about the debate my friend Catherine and I had (see Literature Debate
column2: Part 1) and why classics are worth our time and why they should be read. My agrument is pretty simplistic
column3: but
column4: I hope
column5: convincing. Just the fact that these books have survived and are still being read to the present day
column6: seems a good reason to read them. They've been around for hundreds and hundreds of years and in some cases thousands of years
column7: and despite their age people still read them
column8: and love and learn from them. There must be something special in these books that makes them survive. Our surroundings now are so vastly different from when these books were written
column9: that it seems that what these books hold in them that makes them survive is beyond the material part of our life. The one common bond between the lives led by the characters in the stories and ours now is our humanity
column10: and surely core human nature and emotions cannot change so easily as our surroundings can. The reason these books are read and people can relate to the characters is because the characters are human
column11: and the author has captured what does not change over the centuries: human emotion and nature. We feel what the character feels because however different the circumstances are that bring them about
column12: the emotions and reactions are ours as well. Another reason why we should read the classics is for the sake of history and perspective. If we read only modern books then we would be restricted by our narrow-mindedness. Reading books written in the past gives us a different perspective to look through. We begin to see our life now
column13: not for itself
column14: but as it has been affected by everything that has gone before us. We begin to see the failure of our modern age and see what we have come from. The majority of classic literature comes from a time when there was no television
column15: radio
column16: or computer. There was more time to focus on life and humanity and many other things. Shouldn't we listen to what these authors have to say
column17: they who took the time to write and study well? Or should we lock ourselves up to our own time
column18: which is but a drop in a bucket of water? The age we live in now is one of action and immediate reward with the least amount of work possible to get there. The books written now are written to an audience which wants read a book and have it fly past like a movie. We have a short attention span which leaves little time for character development. Good movies are great things
column19: but a book is not a movie and a movie is not a book. So why should a book imitate a movie? Those who write today shouldn't write movies
column22: or else our age will be forgotten. There is a Latin proverb that goes something like this
column23: ""The man who does not read good books has no advantage over the man who cannot read them."""

column1: 2004-05-11вЂЋAs a big Lord of the Rings fan I was really excited when the Fellowship of the Ring came out in theaters. column2: I'm trying to figure out how I feel about them. Overall I think Peter Jackson did a very good job.  But
column3: there were some things I was disapointed with.   1. Of course a film based on Tolkien's books would have to be violent. But the film should not concentrate on the violence of the book at the expence of the dialogue. I don't think that was so much the case in The Fellowship of the Ring
column4: but in The Two Towers and The Return of the King it was more of a factor.  2. The Return of the King left out several major parts that I think should have been left in. The fact that Denathor was using the Palantir adds a different demention to his maddness. And especially the ending with the shire being untouched. That really needs to be fixed.  3. Faramier has always been one of my favorite characters. I admit that I like him even better than Aragorn. So I was very annoyed when The Two Towers came out and he was portrayed as the exact image of his brother. The same greed and weakness infested the spotless character of Faramier. Is someone less interesting because they're good?  So those are some of my disapointments. Here are some of the  many  good parts.  1. The casting was  excellent . All the cast fit into the characters they played perfectly. The acting
column5: too
column6: was excellent.  2. The scenery and sets were just as a reader would imagine them. 3. Gollum was the star of the show! Really he was portrayed wonderfully. The Gollum and Smeagol conversation in The Two Towers was perfect. He was supurbly done.

column1: 2004-05-22вЂЋThis evening we had a piano recital. I didn't do particularly well
column2: but then again I absolutely hate the piece I played
column3: and it is not the sort of piece that does credit to anyone's ability whether it is played well or not. If you've never been to or played at a typical piano rectial you might want to consider it. It's not that it's an informing of enlighting experience
column4: but it's just so down-to-earth homely. They always start out with the little kids who sit on the piano bench and their feet don't touch the floor. They loudly plunk out some melody or other
column5: usually things like 'Lullaby' or 'Detective Song'. Then there will be some brilliant youngster who makes you feel ashamed of yourself because he plays so well at his age. Then there's a string of twelve-year-olds with varying performances. After that comes the teenagers who barely nod to the applauding audience and slump off the stage without bowing. (There are some exceptions to that rule--my friend
column6: Jenna and myself
column7: for example.) Finally it ends with the old student who plays a very impressive piece from memory. This is the general pattern for typial recitals.

column1: 2004-06-03вЂЋJust as I posted Part 1 on this topic
column2: I thought up more to say. Compare our expectations of teenagers now to the expectations of teenagers throughout history. It is really only in the latter part of the 20th century that these low expectations developed. Why? I donГўв‚¬в„ўt have an answer to that one yet. My general guess would be the increased emphasis on the individual
column3: and less emphasis on the family. I draw my idea of what the teenager was in history from my literary experience. For (and this is a whole other topic to blog about) literature and history cannot be separated. IГўв‚¬в„ўll one of my favorite books as an example.  Sense and Sensibility
column4: by Jane Austen
column5: is really a study of two teenagers. Two sisters
column6: Elinor and Marianne Dashwood
column7: are the central figures. They are 18 (or 17? Not sure about that one.) and 15 respectively. Both
column8: though they behave very differently
column9: are young women of character
column10: principles
column11: intelligence
column12: and learning. They respect their mother
column13: and show support for her in their time of need as a family. The plot of the book (as in all good books) is driven by their different characters
column14: and combination of flaws and virtues. Their faults
column15: and now that I think of it the faults of many teenagers in classic literature
column16: result mainly from a deficiency in experience. I donГўв‚¬в„ўt know if IГўв‚¬в„ўm making any sense. But anyway
column17: my point is that the way we view teenagers today is not the same as before. Many great man and women
column18: Jane Austen
column19: James Madison
column22: and many more showed their potential during their teenage years. From the way teens are expected to behave now it is hard to imagine them having any potential at all.

column1: 2004-06-03вЂЋI was just thinking about the general expectations of the average teenager. And the more I think about it I realize how low these expectations are. I canГўв‚¬в„ўt give any exact examples of what I mean

column1: 2004-06-09вЂЋThe phrase 'be yourself' is a very popular one. But I find it ironic that those who want to be themselves often resort to things that make them even more like others. That may be confusing
column2: but here's what I mean: Those who want to stand out
column3: or be different often resort to many piercings
column4: tattoos
column5: dyeing and styling their hair in odd ways
column6: and other unnatural things like that. However
column7: my point is that those things only make them stand out  less . For I can walk through the mall and see three people with green hair
column8: or multiple piercings and it only detracts from what is  really original : their natural selves. When you dye your hair green it takes away your own unique color
column9: and it puts you in a class of all the others who have green hair. When you cover yourself with tattoos it detracts attention from your God-given natural looks to something made by man. So in the end those who claim that they are 'being themselves' are really only hiding what is theirs alone. Am I making any sense?

column1: 2004-06-17вЂЋYesterday I finished  Lord of the Flies
column2: by William Golding. This is a phenomenal book. It is the story of some schoolboys who are wrecked on an uninhabited island. It is the tale of how they start an organized tribe
column3: but how they quickly succumb to savagery and anarchy. ItГўв‚¬в„ўs a book about original sin. It shows that
column4: however young we are
column5: when the security of all we have known in the civilized world
column6: the supervision of those in authority
column7: and the rules that govern our old life are taken away
column8: our nature is one of darkness and violence
column9: of sin and corruption. The boys start out by electing a chief
column10: Ralph
column11: and setting up some basic rules. The start a signal fire so that a ship passing by might know of their presence and rescue them. Soon all of the boys except Ralph and another boy called Piggy and a few others
column12: forget the importance of the fire and start doing what they will. Slowly all that was organized and all the rules are forgotten by most of the boys. There is a boy called Jack who is very violent and wants to be the leader and do nothing but hunt the animals on the island. Eventually almost all of the boys side with him and forget the fire. The fire dies out because there are not enough boys to keep it up who are willing to do so. The breaking up of the rules hurts the boys
column13: even though most donГўв‚¬в„ўt recognize this
column14: because it lessens their chance of rescue. Here is an interesting part:  Гўв‚¬Е“The rules!Гўв‚¬ shouted Ralph. Гўв‚¬Е“YouГўв‚¬в„ўre breaking the rules!Гўв‚¬ Гўв‚¬Е“Who cares?Гўв‚¬ Ralph summoned his wits. Гўв‚¬Е“Because the rules are the only thing weГўв‚¬в„ўve got!Гўв‚¬ But Jack was shouting against him.  I think this is something that is very relevant to our society today. Today
column15: our culture wants to have as few rules as possible. The attitude is Гўв‚¬Лњdo whatever you want so long as it doesnГўв‚¬в„ўt hurt anyone elseГўв‚¬в„ў. But one thing we donГўв‚¬в„ўt see is how much doing what we want does hurt us. It may hurt us and others around us where we canГўв‚¬в„ўt see it. But just because we canГўв‚¬в„ўt see where it hurts us
column16: doesnГўв‚¬в„ўt mean it isnГўв‚¬в„ўt there. This is because our nature is one that is dark and evil. It is so bad
column17: and so ingrained into ourselves that we donГўв‚¬в„ўt see how awful it is. We are never free of our nature. So left to ourselves how can we do anything that doesnГўв‚¬в„ўt hurt us and others around us? In that way
column18: the rules set by God and by civilization give us freedom. We then have the freedom to choose right or wrong. These boys
column19: when they no longer had grown ups to enforce these rules
column22: and murderous. They killed each other. I remember
column23: after reading the first chapter or two
column24: thinking  how could they become that bad
column26: theyГўв‚¬в„ўre just kids ? But they did become that bad. But the fact is that they didnГўв‚¬в„ўt  become  that bad
column28: only the rules gave them the freedom to choose what was right. In many good books itГўв‚¬в„ўs the characters that drive the plot. ItГўв‚¬в„ўs not like that in this book. Though the main characters are well developed
column29: itГўв‚¬в„ўs the idea behind the story that drives this book. Novels are one of many wonderful ways to communicate an idea. They have the ability to do it so subtly
column31: too. Golding did a wonderful thing when he wrote this book. ItГўв‚¬в„ўs violent
column34: but itГўв‚¬в„ўs  good . ItГўв‚¬в„ўs good because it communicates truth. All that is violent and gory in this book is for the purpose of relating something that is true about our selves. And even the fact that man can communicate something in this way shows that he is made in the image of God. That which is good in man
column35: is the part of man that is GodГўв‚¬в„ўs image. The creativity of man is of God. The sinfulness of our nature is not of God
column37: but there is the wonderful hope that God has provided for a way for salvation from that nature. (IsnГўв‚¬в„ўt it great how much there is to think about in a good book? I think churches should have Literary Discussion Groups!) By the way

column1: 2004-06-28вЂЋThere has been a great amount of discussion due to the proposal of some of the Southern Baptists to remove their children from the public schools. At this point I will not argue my entire side of the issue

column3: but I just want to briefly reflect on one point. One reason people give to keep their children in the public schools is so that they can be a light to the world. They consider putting their children in a Christian school or home-schooling them
column4: as hiding them under a bushel. Or they say it would be ignoring the Great Commission to put them there
column5: unable to interact with and shine among non-Christians. This is not true because:  1. The whole point of school is to get an education; it is a time of learning and development on the childГўв‚¬в„ўs part. The main point of going to school is not to evangelize. Also
column6: putting children in a Christian school
column7: or home-schooling them
column8: does not mean that they have no opportunities to interact with non-Christians.  2. Putting a child in the public school from Kindergarten through high school means they will be spending much of the day in the secular world
column9: more than they spend in Church
column10: Sunday School
column11: youth group
column12: prayer and devotions put together. Without the time spent in a Christian environment how are they to evangelize well
column13: anyway?  3. Putting Christian children in public schools brings in a lot of competition for the parents. They have to compete with their childrenГўв‚¬в„ўs exposure to all the enticing temptations of the secular world. Even if Christian children avoid the temptations
column14: they will certainly not try to evangelize and mark themselves out
column15: risking seclusion. Peer pressure like that from Kindergarten is not healthy for a young ChristianГўв‚¬в„ўs growth
column16: and if a Christian child is weak
column17: how can we expect strong evangelism?  4. From a scientific perspective
column18: it is unwise to put Christians in the public schools because it will only make them weaker in one part of evangelism: the public schools teach the theory of evolution as a fact rather than a theory. Even if they refer to it as a theory they offer no alternatives. Christians
column19: then
column20: will have no knowledge of the Creationist theory
column21: and will not know all of the scientific evidence against evolution and for Creationism. This will leave them very weak
column22: and ignorant. And if they try to evangelize later on
column23: this could be a very weak point.  5. The triumphs and down falls in Christian History are not taught in the public schools
column24: nor are they taught in Sunday Schools. (The Sunday Schools have a hard enough time combating with what the kids are bombarded with in the public schools to even think about teaching Christian History.) Where are Christian children ever to learn about their heritage? The children of the secular world will have more training in their beliefs than the children of God. And then GodГўв‚¬в„ўs children will have their impressions of History
column28: Music and Math molded by those of the secular world. (God isnГўв‚¬в„ўt the Lord of all His creation for nothing. God is present in all areas of learning
column29: and we should teach our children accordingly.) The children of God will surely suffer for it.   Well
column31: but I think that it shows why I believe that
column33: putting our Christian children in the public schools will only weaken their light
